name: Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank International, 573 U.S. 208 (2014)
holding: Two-step framework for 101: (1) determine if claim directed to abstract idea; (2) if so, check for inventive concept transforming abstract idea into patent-eligible application

name: Electric Power Group v. Alsthom, 830 F.3d 1350 (Fed. Cir. 2016)
holding: "collecting/analyzing/displaying data" is abstract even when performed on a network; no concrete mechanism for implementation defeats patent eligibility

name: Two-Way Media v. Comcast, 874 F.3d 1329 (Fed. Cir. 2017)
holding: Functional claims without concrete mechanism are abstract; reciting results of an abstract process rather than specific means to achieve them fails Step 2A

name: BSG Tech v. BuySeasons, 899 F.3d 1281 (Fed. Cir. 2018)
holding: Conventional computer components (databases, networks, processors) don't provide inventive concept under Alice Step 2B even if well-understood components are combined in novel ways

name: Santarus v. Par Pharmaceutical, 694 F.3d 1344 (Fed. Cir. 2012)
holding: Negative limitations require written description support; patentee must affirmatively disclose why excluded embodiments are excluded

name: LizardTech v. Earth Resource Mapping, 424 F.3d 1336 (Fed. Cir. 2005)
holding: Cannot claim genus when only a single species is disclosed in the specification; claim scope must be commensurate with what inventor possessed

name: Ariad v. Eli Lilly, 598 F.3d 1336 (Fed. Cir. 2010, en banc)
holding: Specification must demonstrate possession of the full scope of the claimed invention; broad functional claims require correspondingly broad written description

name: Interval Licensing v. AOL, 766 F.3d 1364 (Fed. Cir. 2014)
holding: Inconsistent or ambiguous terminology throughout the specification renders claims indefinite; terms must have consistent meaning across all uses

name: Nautilus v. Biosig, 572 U.S. 898 (2014)
holding: "Reasonable certainty" standard for definiteness: claims must inform, with reasonable certainty, those skilled in the art about the scope of the invention

name: Festo Corp. v. Shoketsu Kinzoku Kogyo Kabushiki Co., 535 U.S. 722 (2002)
holding: Prosecution history estoppel limits doctrine of equivalents after narrowing amendments; surrender presumed unless narrowing amendment was for reasons unrelated to patentability

name: Prism Technologies v. T-Mobile, 928 F.3d 1364 (Fed. Cir. 2019)
holding: Token-based authentication is abstract; providing access to network resources using tokens does not transform the abstract idea into patent-eligible subject matter

name: Burstiq v. Dentaquest
holding: Blockchain-based health records are abstract; adding "on a blockchain" does not save abstract ideas from ineligibility under Alice Step 2B

name: KSR International v. Teleflex, 550 U.S. 398 (2007)
holding: Motivation-to-combine framework for obviousness; market pressure, design needs, or finite identified solutions create motivation to combine prior art references even without explicit teaching
